en.extract:
The Cocos booby (Sula brewsteri), formerly known as Brewster's booby, is a species of booby in the family Sulidae. It was formerly considered a subspecies of brown booby, but it was split by the American Ornithological Society in 2024 due to morphological and behavioural differences. It is native to the east and central Pacific Ocean.


Taxonomy
The Cocos booby was first described by Nathaniel Stickney Goss in 1888 based on birds collected on San Pedro Mártir Island in the Gulf of California. He gave the birds the Latin name Sula brewsteri after his friend and fellow ornithologist William Brewster. In 1944, the ornithologist Alexander Wetmore placed Brewster's booby as a subspecies of brown booby. In 2024 a proposal to the American Ornithological Society to return to treating Brewster's booby as a distinct species was passed unanimously. Due to the changes of all North American bird names including the names of people, the name for the species was also changed from Brewster's booby to Cocos booby, after Cocos Island off Costa Rica, where the species breeds.
Cocos booby currently has either two or three subspecies depending on the taxonomic authority. The subspecies are:

S. b. brewsteri (Goss, 1888) - Breeds on islands in the Gulf of California and the Pacific Ocean off the Baja California Peninsula of Mexico.
S. b. etesiaca (Thayer & Bangs, 1905) - Breeds on islands in the Pacific Ocean off of Central America and Colombia.
S. b. nesiotes (Heller & Snodgrass, 1901) - Breeds on Clipperton Island.


Description
The most immediately obvious difference from the related brown booby is the male's whitish face, varying in size by subspecies. Females of all subspecies of Cocos booby also possess a white forehead. Males of the subspecies S. b. nesiotes have the most white, with a fully white head and neck. Males of the nominate subspecies have a white face, and a pale brown neck, while males of the subspecies S. b. etesiaca only have white on their foreheads. Males also have a grey bill, rather than the yellow bill of male brown booby, and females have a pinker bill than female brown booby.


Behaviour

Like other boobies, the Cocos booby is a colonial breeder, and nests on islands. When the Cocos booby comes in contact with its relative, interbreeding appears to be rare. Observations on Wake Island found that male and female Cocos booby consistently paired with each other in large brown booby colonies. Other observations found female Cocos booby among only brown boobies did not pair, while instances of hybridization have only been reported at colonies with male Cocos booby and no female Cocos booby. At least two instances of hybridization between Cocos booby and brown booby are known, one from Midway Atoll in 2020 and the other from Nakanokamishima Island from 2012 to 2014, where a chick was successfully reared.


Distribution
Cocos booby are found in the East Pacific Ocean, breeding in Costa Rica, Nicaragua, Mexico, French Overseas Territories, Colombia, and the United States. It is also found offshore of Guatemala, El Salvador, and Panama. In recent years, Cocos booby has dispersed and is now found in the Central and West Pacific in Japan and Hawaii where they have now bred. They are only recent resident breeders in California, first breeding in the Channel Islands in 2017, and now regularly occur as far north on the Pacific coast as Vancouver. Large colonies exist on the islands of Clipperton Island, San Pedro Mártir Island, Isla Espíritu Santo, Isla Isabel, Islas Marietas, Socorro Island, Isla del Caño, Gorgona Island, and Cocos Island. Recently, breeding has occurred on Palmyra Atoll, Wake Island, Midway Atoll, and Moku Manu in the United States Minor Outlying Islands and Hawaii.


Notes


References


External links

 Data related to Sula brewsteri at Wikispecies

The '''Cocos booby''' ('''''Sula brewsteri'''''), formerly known as '''Brewster's booby''', is a species of [[booby]] in the family [[Sulidae]]. It was formerly considered a subspecies of [[brown booby]], but it was split by the [[American Ornithological Society]] in 2024 due to morphological and behavioural differences.<ref name="AOS">{{cite web |title=2024-B-2: Treat Sula brewsteri as a separate species from Brown Booby S. leucogaster |url=https://americanornithology.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/07/2024-B-final.pdf |website=American Ornithological Society |access-date=17 October 2024}}</ref> It is native to the east and central [[Pacific Ocean]].

== Taxonomy ==
The Cocos booby was first described by [[Nathaniel Stickney Goss]] in 1888 based on birds collected on [[San Pedro Mártir Island]] in the [[Gulf of California]].<ref name="Goss">{{cite journal |last1=Goss |first1=Nathaniel Stickney |title=New and rare birds found breeding on the San Pedro Martir Isle. |journal=[[The Auk]] |date=1888 |volume=5 |page=242 |url=https://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/auk/v005n03/p0240-p0244.pdf |access-date=17 October 2024}}</ref> He gave the birds the Latin name '''''Sula brewsteri''''' after his friend and fellow ornithologist [[William Brewster (ornithologist)|William Brewster]].<ref name="Goss" /> In 1944, the ornithologist [[Alexander Wetmore]] placed Brewster's booby as a subspecies of brown booby. In 2024 a proposal to the American Ornithological Society to return to treating Brewster's booby as a distinct species was passed unanimously.<ref name="AOS" /> Due to the changes of all North American bird names including the names of people, the name for the species was also changed from Brewster's booby to Cocos booby, after [[Cocos Island]] off [[Costa Rica]], where the species breeds.<ref name="AOS" />

Cocos booby currently has either two or three subspecies depending on the [[Taxonomic authorities|taxonomic authority]].<ref name="South America">{{cite web |title=Treat Sula brewsteri as a separate species from Brown Booby ''S. leucogaster'' (Part A), and if Part A passes, establish English name for ''Sula brewsteri'' (Part B) |url=https://www.museum.lsu.edu/~Remsen/SACCprop995.htm |website=Louisiana State University Museum |access-date=17 October 2024}}</ref><ref name="Avibase">{{cite web |title=Cocos Booby |url=https://avibase.bsc-eoc.org/species.jsp?lang=EN&avibaseid=AD2B8E994C107C6D&sec=summary |website=Avibase |access-date=17 October 2024}}</ref>{{NoteTag|The [[Clements Checklist]] does not include ''S. b. nesiotes''}} The subspecies are:
* ''S. b. brewsteri'' <small>(Goss, 1888)</small> - Breeds on islands in the [[Gulf of California]] and the [[Pacific Ocean]] off the [[Baja California peninsula|Baja California Peninsula]] of [[Mexico]].
* ''S. b. etesiaca'' <small>([[John Thayer (ornithologist)|Thayer]] & [[Outram Bangs|Bangs]], 1905)</small> - Breeds on islands in the Pacific Ocean off of [[Central America]] and [[Colombia]].
* ''S. b. nesiotes'' <small>(Heller & Snodgrass, 1901)</small> - Breeds on [[Clipperton Island]].

== Description ==
The most immediately obvious difference from the related brown booby is the male's whitish face, varying in size by subspecies. Females of all subspecies of Cocos booby also possess a white forehead.<ref name="South America" /> Males of the subspecies ''S. b. nesiotes'' have the most white, with a fully white head and neck.<ref name="South America" /> Males of the nominate subspecies have a white face, and a pale brown neck, while males of the subspecies ''S. b. etesiaca'' only have white on their foreheads.<ref name="South America" /> Males also have a grey bill, rather than the yellow bill of male brown booby, and females have a pinker bill than female brown booby.<ref name="South America" />

== Behaviour ==
[[File: Sula leucogaster brewsteri (cropped).jpg|thumb|right|''S. b. brewsteri'' with chick]]
Like other boobies, the Cocos booby is a [[Colony (biology)|colonial breeder]], and nests on islands. When the Cocos booby comes in contact with its relative, interbreeding appears to be rare. Observations on [[Wake Island]] found that male and female Cocos booby consistently paired with each other in large brown booby colonies.<ref name="South America" /> Other observations found female Cocos booby among only brown boobies did not pair, while instances of [[Hybridization (biology)|hybridization]] have only been reported at colonies with male Cocos booby and no female Cocos booby.<ref name="South America" /> At least two instances of hybridization between Cocos booby and brown booby are known, one from [[Midway Atoll]] in 2020 and the other from [[Yaeyama Islands|Nakanokamishima Island]] from 2012 to 2014, where a chick was successfully reared.<ref name="South America" />

== Distribution ==
Cocos booby are found in the [[East Pacific Ocean]], breeding in [[Costa Rica]], [[Nicaragua]], [[Mexico]], [[Overseas France|French Overseas Territories]], [[Colombia]], and the [[United States]].<ref name="CWP" /> It is also found offshore of [[Guatemala]], [[El Salvador]], and [[Panama]]. In recent years, Cocos booby has dispersed and is now found in the Central and West Pacific in [[Japan]] and [[Hawaii]] where they have now bred.<ref name="South America" /> They are only recent resident breeders in [[California]], first breeding in the [[Channel Islands (California)|Channel Islands]] in 2017, and now regularly occur as far north on the [[Pacific Northwest|Pacific coast]] as [[Vancouver]].<ref name="CWP">{{cite web |title=Red-foot, Blue-foot, Cocos, Nazca: Tropical seabirds moving into the Pacific Northwest |url=https://thecottonwoodpost.net/tag/cocos-booby/ |website=The Cottonwood Post |date=20 August 2024 |access-date=18 October 2024}}</ref> Large colonies exist on the islands of Clipperton Island, San Pedro Mártir Island, [[Isla Espíritu Santo]], [[Isla Isabel National Park|Isla Isabel]], [[Islas Marietas National Park|Islas Marietas]], [[Socorro Island]], [[Isla del Caño]], [[Gorgona Island (Colombia)|Gorgona Island]], and Cocos Island. Recently, breeding has occurred on [[Palmyra Atoll]], [[Wake Island]], [[Midway Atoll]], and [[Moku Manu]] in the [[United States Minor Outlying Islands]] and Hawaii.<ref name="South America" />
